articles.headline: Government Unveils National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS)
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the launch of the National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS) aimed at boosting industrial competitiveness and creating a future-ready workforce.
• NMAMS will focus on integrating cutting-edge technologies like AI, IoT, and robotics into manufacturing processes, coupled with skill development programs for youth.
• Key objectives include enhancing productivity, promoting innovation, attracting foreign investment, and establishing Centers of Excellence across various industrial corridors.
• The mission targets a significant increase in the manufacturing sector's contribution to GDP and aims to create millions of skilled jobs over the next five years.
• It will operate on a public-private partnership (PPP) model, encouraging industry collaboration with academic institutions and government bodies.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Industrial policy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Skill Development
prelimsPoints: NMAMS aims to integrate AI, IoT, robotics in manufacturing.
Focus on skill development for future industries.
Operates on a Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model.
Key objective: increase manufacturing sector's GDP contribution.
Establishment of Centers of Excellence.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of advanced manufacturing for India's economic growth and global competitiveness.
Analyze the role of skill development in leveraging new technologies for industrial transformation.
Evaluate the potential impact of NMAMS on employment generation and youth empowerment.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in implementing a mission-mode approach for industrial modernization.
Critically assess the PPP model's effectiveness in driving technological innovation and skill development.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration depicting advanced manufacturing robots and skilled workers in a modern factory setting.
articles.trivia: The term 'Industry 4.0' was first coined in Germany in 2011 to describe the fourth industrial revolution.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Amendments to Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India today upheld the constitutional validity of several contentious amendments made to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, in 2023.
• The amendments primarily redefine 'forest' and exempt certain categories of land from the Act's purview, including forest land up to 0.10 hectares for public utility projects and land within 100 km of international borders for strategic projects.
• Environmental groups and petitioners had argued that these amendments diluted the protective scope of the original Act and could lead to large-scale deforestation.
• The court, however, emphasized the need for a balance between environmental protection and developmental imperatives, asserting that the new provisions include adequate safeguards.
• The ruling is expected to expedite infrastructure projects in critical border areas and facilitate certain developmental activities in non-notified forest lands.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court upheld amendments to Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
Amendments redefine 'forest' and exempt certain land categories.
Exemptions include small public utility projects and strategic border projects.
Ruling balances environmental protection with developmental needs.
Original Act passed in 1980.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's judgment on India's environmental conservation efforts and infrastructure development.
Discuss the ongoing debate between environmental protection and developmental needs in the context of forest laws.
Critically evaluate the 'balance' struck by the court and the adequacy of safeguards in the amended Act.
Examine the role of the judiciary in interpreting environmental legislation and its impact on public policy.
Suggest measures to ensure sustainable development while accelerating infrastructure projects, especially in sensitive ecological zones.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a legal book with a backdrop of a dense forest, symbolizing the judiciary's role in environmental law.
articles.trivia: The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, was enacted to check deforestation and ensure that forest land is not diverted for non-forest purposes without prior approval from the Central Government.

articles.headline: India Successfully Concludes 'Bharat Shakti' Tri-Services Exercise in Rajasthan
articles.explainer:
• The fourth edition of 'Bharat Shakti', a massive tri-services live fire and maneuver exercise, concluded successfully at the Pokhran Field Firing Ranges in Rajasthan.
• The exercise showcased the integrated combat capabilities of the Indian Army, Navy, and Air Force, demonstrating synergy in multi-domain operations.
• Key highlights included live demonstrations of indigenous weapon systems, advanced surveillance technologies, and joint logistics operations.
• The event was witnessed by senior defence officials and international observers, underscoring India's growing defence manufacturing prowess and strategic readiness.
• 'Bharat Shakti' aims to validate operational concepts, enhance interoperability, and provide a platform for showcasing 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiatives in defence.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: Bharat Shakti is a tri-services exercise (Army, Navy, Air Force).
Held at Pokhran Field Firing Ranges, Rajasthan.
Focuses on integrated combat capabilities and indigenous weapon systems.
Aims to enhance interoperability and strategic readiness.
Fourth edition of the exercise.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of joint military exercises like 'Bharat Shakti' in enhancing India's defence preparedness and strategic capabilities.
Analyze the role of indigenization in defence manufacturing (Atmanirbhar Bharat) as showcased in such exercises.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in achieving seamless interoperability among India's three armed services.
Evaluate India's position as a regional security provider in the context of its evolving military doctrines and capabilities.
How do such exercises contribute to deterrence and regional stability?
articles.imageDescription: Indian military personnel from Army, Navy, and Air Force conducting a joint exercise with tanks, aircraft, and warships.
articles.trivia: Pokhran is famously known for being the site of India's nuclear weapons tests in 1974 and 1998.

articles.headline: RBI Releases Annual Report on Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) Implementation
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today released its annual report detailing the implementation and enforcement of the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999, for the fiscal year 2024-25.
• The report highlighted trends in foreign exchange transactions, capital flows, and compliance levels among entities operating under FEMA regulations.
• It noted a significant increase in foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows and a stable outward remittances landscape, attributing it to robust regulatory oversight and economic confidence.
• The RBI also outlined key enforcement actions taken against non-compliant entities and proposed minor amendments to further streamline FEMA's provisions.
• This annual review is crucial for policymakers to assess the effectiveness of India's foreign exchange regime and its alignment with global best practices.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Investment models
Government Budgeting
prelimsPoints: RBI releases annual report on FEMA implementation.
FEMA, 1999, regulates foreign exchange transactions.
Report covers trends in FDI, capital flows, and compliance.
RBI proposes minor amendments to FEMA.
FEMA replaced FERA (Foreign Exchange Regulation Act).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of FEMA in regulating India's foreign exchange market and facilitating international trade and investment.
Analyze the trends in FDI and capital flows as highlighted by the RBI report and their implications for the Indian economy.
Evaluate the effectiveness of RBI's regulatory and enforcement mechanisms under FEMA.
Examine the impact of global economic trends and geopolitical events on India's foreign exchange management.
Suggest reforms or policy changes to further liberalize or strengthen India's foreign exchange regime.
articles.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) logo with graphs depicting economic trends in the background.
articles.trivia: FEMA came into effect on June 1, 2000, replacing the more stringent FERA, 1973, to liberalize India's foreign exchange regime.

articles.headline: India-France Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Indo-Pacific Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-France Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming the strong bilateral partnership and focusing on enhanced cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.
• The delegations, led by National Security Advisors from both countries, discussed a wide range of issues including defence collaboration, counter-terrorism, space technology, and civil nuclear cooperation.
• A joint statement highlighted the commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, with specific emphasis on maritime security and joint exercises.
• Both nations reiterated their support for multilateralism and reforms in global institutions, including the UN Security Council.
• The dialogue also explored new avenues for collaboration in emerging technologies and climate change initiatives, solidifying their strategic autonomy partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-France Strategic Dialogue held in New Delhi.
Focus on Indo-Pacific cooperation and maritime security.
Discussed defence, counter-terrorism, space, nuclear energy.
Both nations support UN Security Council reforms.
Led by National Security Advisors.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving strategic partnership between India and France, particularly in the context of Indo-Pacific geopolitics.
Discuss the significance of bilateral cooperation in defence and space technology for India's strategic autonomy.
Examine how India and France are contributing to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
Evaluate the potential for further collaboration in emerging technologies and climate change initiatives.
What are the challenges and opportunities for India-France relations amidst global power shifts?
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and France displayed together, symbolizing bilateral cooperation.
articles.trivia: France was one of the first countries to establish a strategic partnership with India in 1998, following India's nuclear tests.

articles.headline: New Guidelines for Green Hydrogen Production Incentives Released by MNRE
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) issued detailed guidelines for production incentives under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• The incentives aim to reduce the cost of green hydrogen production, making it competitive with fossil fuel-based alternatives and accelerating its adoption across various sectors.
• The guidelines specify eligibility criteria for projects, performance benchmarks, and the mechanism for disbursing financial support to manufacturers of electrolyzers and producers of green hydrogen.
• This move is crucial for positioning India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing significantly to its decarbonization goals.
• Industry stakeholders have welcomed the clarity, expecting a boost in investments and technological advancements in the green hydrogen ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
prelimsPoints: MNRE released guidelines for green hydrogen production incentives.
Aims to reduce green hydrogen production costs.
Incentives for electrolyzer manufacturers and green hydrogen producers.
Part of the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
Goal: India as a global green hydrogen hub.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change mitigation efforts.
Analyze the role of government incentives in fostering the growth of the green hydrogen economy in India.
Examine the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and the potential solutions.
Evaluate India's potential to become a global leader in green hydrogen production and export.
How will the adoption of green hydrogen impact various sectors of the Indian economy?
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic image of a green hydrogen production plant with renewable energy sources like solar panels and wind turbines.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

articles.headline: ISRO Gears Up for Venus Orbiter Mission (Shukrayaan-1) Launch in Mid-2025
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced that preparations for its ambitious Venus Orbiter Mission, 'Shukrayaan-1', are in advanced stages for a mid-2025 launch.
• Shukrayaan-1 aims to study the atmosphere and surface of Venus, focusing on its dense sulfuric acid clouds, volcanic activity, and atmospheric composition.
• The mission will carry several indigenous scientific instruments, including a high-resolution radar and a spectrometer, to gather unprecedented data from Venus.
• This mission marks India's first foray into exploring Venus, building on the success of its Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan).
• The scientific data collected by Shukrayaan-1 is expected to provide crucial insights into planetary evolution and the habitability of extreme environments.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space technology
prelimsPoints: ISRO's Venus Orbiter Mission is named 'Shukrayaan-1'.
Aims to study Venus's atmosphere and surface.
Carries indigenous scientific instruments (radar, spectrometer).
India's first mission to Venus.
Launch targeted for mid-2025.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's Venus Orbiter Mission (Shukrayaan-1) for planetary science and space exploration.
Analyze the role of ISRO in advancing India's technological capabilities and global standing in space research.
Examine the scientific objectives of Shukrayaan-1 and its potential contributions to understanding planetary evolution.
Compare India's approach to interplanetary missions with other major spacefaring nations.
What are the future prospects for India's space exploration programs beyond Mars and Venus?
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of the Shukrayaan-1 spacecraft orbiting Venus, with its scientific instruments deployed.
articles.trivia: Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to its similar size and mass, but it has an extremely hot, toxic atmosphere.

articles.headline: Government to Revamp Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) for Wider Adoption
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced a comprehensive revamp of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to address implementation challenges and accelerate its adoption across the country.
• The revamp focuses on simplifying the user interface, enhancing interoperability between digital health platforms, and strengthening data privacy and security protocols.
• Key initiatives include increased outreach campaigns, training for healthcare providers, and incentives for states to integrate their health systems with ABDM.
• The mission aims to create a seamless online platform for healthcare services, linking patient records, doctors, and healthcare facilities through unique Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA).
• The move is expected to improve accessibility, efficiency, and transparency in India's healthcare delivery system.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: ABDM revamp announced by Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
Aims to simplify UI, enhance interoperability, strengthen data privacy.
Creates Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA).
Goal: seamless online healthcare platform.
Expected to improve healthcare accessibility and efficiency.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of digital health missions like ABDM in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Analyze the challenges faced in the implementation of ABDM and how the proposed revamp aims to address them.
Examine the importance of data privacy and security in a comprehensive digital health ecosystem.
Evaluate the role of technology in improving healthcare access and reducing disparities in India.
Suggest further measures to ensure equitable adoption and effective utilization of digital health services across rural and urban areas.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission logo with interconnected digital health icons.
articles.trivia: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was renamed as Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in September 2021.

articles.headline: Report Highlights Persistent Challenges in Achieving SDG 4 (Quality Education) in India
articles.explainer:
• A recent report by a leading think tank outlined persistent challenges India faces in achieving Sustainable Development Goal 4 (SDG 4) on quality education by 2030.
• The report pointed to issues such as unequal access to quality education, particularly in rural and marginalized communities, inadequate infrastructure, and teacher shortages.
• It emphasized the learning crisis, with many students not achieving foundational literacy and numeracy skills, despite improvements in enrolment rates.
• Recommendations included increased public spending on education, focus on early childhood education, teacher training reforms, and leveraging technology for equitable learning outcomes.
• The findings underscore the need for targeted interventions and robust policy implementation to ensure inclusive and equitable quality education for all.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: Report on SDG 4 (Quality Education) challenges in India.
Identifies unequal access, infrastructure gaps, teacher shortages.
Highlights the learning crisis in foundational skills.
Recommends increased public spending and teacher training.
SDG 4 aims for inclusive and equitable quality education by 2030.
mainsPoints: Discuss the progress and persistent challenges India faces in achieving SDG 4 on quality education.
Analyze the socio-economic factors contributing to unequal access and poor learning outcomes in the Indian education system.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government policies and initiatives (e.g., NEP 2020) in addressing these challenges.
Suggest innovative strategies and policy reforms to improve foundational literacy, numeracy, and overall quality of education.
Examine the role of technology and public-private partnerships in bridging the educational divide in India.
articles.imageDescription: Children studying in a classroom, with a graphic overlay of SDG 4 icon.
articles.trivia: SDG 4 has ten targets, including universal primary and secondary education, early childhood development, technical and vocational skills, and increasing qualified teachers.

articles.headline: India Pushes for Reforms in WTO's Dispute Settlement Mechanism at Geneva Meeting
articles.explainer:
• India reiterated its strong demand for comprehensive reforms of the World Trade Organization's (WTO) Dispute Settlement Mechanism (DSM) at a ministerial meeting in Geneva.
• India emphasized the need for a fully functioning, two-tier dispute settlement system that is accessible, fair, and equitable for all members, especially developing countries.
• The DSM has been largely non-functional since 2019 due to the inability to appoint new members to its Appellate Body, mainly blocked by the United States.
• India's proposal includes restoring the Appellate Body, ensuring judicial independence, and addressing issues of overreach and delays in dispute resolution.
• A reformed DSM is crucial for maintaining a rules-based multilateral trading system and protecting the interests of developing nations.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India advocates for reforms in WTO's Dispute Settlement Mechanism (DSM).
DSM has been non-functional since 2019 due to Appellate Body vacancies.
India demands a two-tier, accessible, fair, and equitable system.
Proposals include restoring Appellate Body and ensuring judicial independence.
WTO is based in Geneva, Switzerland.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a robust Dispute Settlement Mechanism for the effective functioning of the WTO and the global trading system.
Analyze the reasons behind the paralysis of the WTO's Appellate Body and its implications for international trade.
Examine India's stance and proposals for reforming the DSM, particularly from the perspective of developing countries.
Evaluate the challenges and prospects for achieving consensus on WTO reforms amidst differing interests of member states.
How does a weakened WTO impact India's trade interests and its pursuit of a rules-based international order?
articles.imageDescription: The World Trade Organization (WTO) logo with flags of member countries in the background.
articles.trivia: The WTO was established in 1995, building on the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) which had been in existence since 1948.

articles.headline: Coastal Erosion Poses Growing Threat to Indian Coastlines: Ministry of Earth Sciences Report
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES) revealed an increasing threat of coastal erosion along significant stretches of India's coastline.
• The report, based on satellite imagery and ground surveys, identified West Bengal, Odisha, Kerala, and Gujarat as particularly vulnerable states.
• Factors contributing to erosion include rising sea levels, increased frequency and intensity of cyclones, anthropogenic activities like sand mining, and climate change.
• The MoES recommended integrated coastal zone management plans, nature-based solutions (e.g., mangrove plantation), and strict enforcement of environmental regulations.
• The findings highlight the urgent need for proactive measures to protect coastal communities, ecosystems, and infrastructure from the adverse impacts of erosion.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: MoES report highlights growing coastal erosion in India.
West Bengal, Odisha, Kerala, Gujarat are highly vulnerable states.
Causes: sea level rise, cyclones, sand mining, climate change.
Recommendations: ICZM plans, nature-based solutions (mangroves).
India has a coastline of over 7,500 km.
mainsPoints: Analyze the major causes and consequences of coastal erosion along the Indian coastline.
Discuss the socio-economic and ecological impacts of coastal erosion on vulnerable communities and ecosystems.
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing coastal management strategies and suggest integrated approaches for mitigation.
Examine the role of climate change and anthropogenic activities in exacerbating coastal erosion in India.
What measures can be taken to enhance the resilience of India's coastal regions against future climatic events?
articles.imageDescription: A drone shot of an eroding coastline with waves crashing against the shore, showing visible land loss.
articles.trivia: Mangrove forests are highly effective in preventing coastal erosion and acting as natural barriers against storm surges.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Strengthening India's Data Protection Framework
articles.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Information Technology submitted its report, recommending further strengthening of India's data protection framework, particularly concerning cross-border data flows and data localization.
• The committee emphasized the need for robust mechanisms to protect citizens' personal data, especially in the wake of increasing digitalization and cyber threats.
• Recommendations included a clearer definition of 'critical personal data', stricter penalties for data breaches, and establishing an independent Data Protection Authority with adequate powers.
• The report also suggested a review of the existing Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, to align it with evolving technological advancements and international best practices.
• This move is crucial for building trust in the digital economy and safeguarding individual privacy rights.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Cyber security
Role of non-state actors in security challenges
prelimsPoints: Parliamentary panel recommends strengthening data protection.
Focus on cross-border data flows and data localization.
Suggests clear definition of 'critical personal data'.
Recommends independent Data Protection Authority.
Review of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a robust data protection framework for India's digital economy and national security.
Analyze the key recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee regarding data protection, including cross-border data flows and data localization.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive data protection law in a diverse and rapidly digitalizing country like India.
Examine the balance between individual privacy rights, state surveillance, and business interests in the context of data governance.
Suggest how India's data protection law can align with global standards while addressing unique domestic concerns.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of data flowing through digital networks, with a shield icon representing data protection.
articles.trivia: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, was enacted after years of debate and multiple draft versions, following the Supreme Court's 'Right to Privacy' judgment in 2017.

articles.headline: Government Reviews Initiatives for Promoting Millets in Public Distribution System (PDS)
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution conducted a high-level review of initiatives aimed at integrating millets into the Public Distribution System (PDS) across various states.
• The review assessed the progress of procurement, storage, and distribution of millets, particularly jowar, bajra, and ragi, to enhance nutritional security.
• Challenges identified included supply chain management, consumer acceptance in some regions, and the need for standardized processing facilities.
• The government plans to expand the pilot projects and increase awareness campaigns about the nutritional benefits of millets, especially in regions with high malnutrition.
• This push aligns with the International Year of Millets (IYM) 2023 objectives and India's commitment to food security and diversified agriculture.
gsSubTopics: Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
articles.source: Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution
prelimsPoints: Review of millet integration into PDS.
Focus on jowar, bajra, ragi for nutritional security.
Challenges: supply chain, consumer acceptance, processing facilities.
Aims to expand pilot projects and awareness campaigns.
Aligned with International Year of Millets (IYM) 2023.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of integrating millets into the Public Distribution System for enhancing food and nutritional security in India.
Analyze the challenges involved in the procurement, storage, and distribution of millets through the PDS network.
Evaluate the potential benefits of promoting millet consumption for public health and agricultural sustainability.
Examine government initiatives and policies aimed at diversifying cropping patterns and supporting millet farmers.
Suggest measures to overcome consumer preference issues and ensure wider acceptance of millets in dietary habits.
articles.imageDescription: Various types of millets displayed in bowls, with PDS ration cards in the background.
articles.trivia: India is the largest producer of millets in the world, accounting for over 40% of global production.

articles.headline: India to Host International Conference on Traditional Medicine Systems
articles.explainer:
• India announced its plan to host a major International Conference on Traditional Medicine Systems later this year, inviting experts and practitioners from around the globe.
• The conference aims to foster global collaboration in research, standardization, and integration of traditional medicine (like Ayurveda, Yoga, Unani, Siddha, Homoeopathy - AYUSH) into modern healthcare.
• Discussions will focus on evidence-based practices, regulatory frameworks, quality control, and intellectual property rights related to traditional knowledge.
• This initiative underscores India's leadership in promoting holistic health approaches and sharing its rich heritage of traditional medicine with the world.
• The event is expected to facilitate knowledge exchange and explore pathways for the global acceptance and utilization of traditional healing practices.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: India to host International Conference on Traditional Medicine Systems.
Aims for global collaboration in AYUSH research and integration.
Focus on evidence-based practices, quality control, IPR.
Underscores India's leadership in traditional medicine.
AYUSH stands for Ayurveda, Yoga, Unani, Siddha, Homoeopathy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of India's traditional medicine systems (AYUSH) in contributing to global health and well-being.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in integrating traditional medicine into conventional healthcare systems.
Examine the importance of standardization, research, and evidence-based validation for the global acceptance of AYUSH practices.
Evaluate India's role in promoting traditional medicine on international platforms and its implications for cultural diplomacy.
Suggest policy measures to protect traditional knowledge and ensure ethical practices in the commercialization of traditional medicine.
articles.imageDescription: A collage of symbols representing various traditional medicine systems like Ayurveda, Yoga, and Unani.
articles.trivia: The World Health Organization (WHO) established its Global Centre for Traditional Medicine in Jamnagar, Gujarat, India, in 2022.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Discussion Paper Highlights Urban Planning Challenges in Indian Megacities
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog released a discussion paper outlining the critical urban planning challenges faced by India's rapidly growing megacities, including issues of sustainable infrastructure and governance.
• The paper identified problems such as unplanned growth, inadequate public transport, housing shortages, waste management crises, and increasing air pollution.
• It stressed the urgent need for integrated urban planning, leveraging smart city technologies, and empowering local urban bodies with greater financial and administrative autonomy.
• Recommendations included promoting green urbanism, developing robust disaster resilience plans, and fostering citizen participation in urban governance.
• The discussion paper aims to solicit feedback from stakeholders to formulate a comprehensive national urban policy framework.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog paper on urban planning challenges in megacities.
Identifies unplanned growth, transport, housing, waste, pollution.
Recommends integrated urban planning, smart city tech, empowered local bodies.
Promotes green urbanism and disaster resilience.
Aims for a national urban policy framework.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted challenges of rapid urbanization and unplanned growth in Indian megacities.
Analyze the role of urban planning and governance in ensuring sustainable and livable urban environments.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government initiatives (e.g., Smart Cities Mission) in addressing urban infrastructure and service delivery gaps.
Examine the importance of citizen participation and local body empowerment for effective urban management.
Suggest innovative policy solutions and best practices from around the world to tackle India's urban planning issues.
articles.imageDescription: A sprawling Indian megacity skyline with a mix of modern buildings and congested areas, symbolizing urban challenges.
articles.trivia: By 2050, over half of India's population is projected to live in urban areas, making urban planning a critical policy area.

articles.headline: India-Sri Lanka Fisheries Talks Remain Inconclusive on Persistent Issue
articles.explainer:
• The latest round of bilateral talks between India and Sri Lanka on the long-standing fisheries dispute in the Palk Strait concluded without a definitive breakthrough.
• The talks focused on issues of illegal fishing by Indian trawlers in Sri Lankan waters, damage to marine ecosystems, and the livelihood concerns of Sri Lankan fishermen.
• India reiterated its commitment to finding a humane and practical solution, proposing joint patrolling and alternative livelihood schemes for Indian fishermen.
• Sri Lanka maintained its demand for an end to bottom trawling and emphasized the need to protect its marine resources and the livelihoods of its northern fishermen.
• Despite ongoing diplomatic efforts, the issue remains a significant irritant in bilateral relations, impacting the lives of thousands of fishing communities.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-Sri Lanka fisheries talks remain inconclusive.
Dispute in Palk Strait over Indian trawlers in Sri Lankan waters.
India proposed joint patrolling, alternative livelihood schemes.
Sri Lanka demands end to bottom trawling.
Issue affects bilateral relations and fishermen livelihoods.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and complexities of the India-Sri Lanka fisheries dispute in the Palk Strait.
Discuss the socio-economic and environmental implications of bottom trawling and illegal fishing in shared waters.
Evaluate the various diplomatic and economic measures taken by both countries to resolve the dispute.
Examine the humanitarian aspect of the issue, focusing on the livelihoods of fishing communities on both sides.
Suggest sustainable and equitable solutions to address the fisheries conflict, considering marine conservation and regional stability.
articles.imageDescription: Fishing boats in the Palk Strait, with maps showing the disputed maritime boundary between India and Sri Lanka.
articles.trivia: The Palk Strait is a strait between the Tamil Nadu state of India and the Jaffna District of the Northern Province of the island nation of Sri Lanka.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog to Develop National Gender Equality Index for Policy Tracking
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog announced its plan to develop a comprehensive National Gender Equality Index to systematically track India's progress on various parameters of gender equality.
• The index will measure performance across key domains such as health, education, economic participation, political representation, and protection against violence.
• It aims to provide a data-driven framework for states and union territories to identify gaps, formulate targeted policies, and monitor the impact of gender-specific interventions.
• The initiative aligns with India's commitment to Sustainable Development Goal 5 (Gender Equality) and will serve as a crucial tool for evidence-based policymaking.
• This move is expected to foster greater accountability and accelerate efforts towards achieving gender parity across all sectors.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog developing National Gender Equality Index.
Measures progress on health, education, economy, politics, safety.
Provides data-driven framework for states/UTs.
Aligns with SDG 5 (Gender Equality).
Aims for greater accountability and gender parity.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a National Gender Equality Index in measuring and accelerating India's progress towards gender parity.
Analyze the key domains that such an index should cover and the indicators necessary for a comprehensive assessment of gender equality.
Evaluate the challenges in collecting reliable gender-disaggregated data and ensuring its effective utilization for policymaking.
Examine how this index can contribute to evidence-based policymaking and foster greater accountability among states and union territories.
Suggest complementary policy interventions and societal changes required to achieve true gender equality in India beyond mere statistical improvements.
articles.imageDescription: A silhouette of a diverse group of people, symbolizing gender equality and inclusivity, with a bar graph showing progress.
articles.trivia: The Gender Inequality Index (GII) is an index for measurement of gender disparity that was introduced in 2010 by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).

articles.headline: India Proposes Global AI Governance Summit, Emphasizes Ethical AI Development
articles.explainer:
• India formally proposed hosting a Global AI Governance Summit in late 2025, aiming to bring together nations to discuss a unified framework for ethical AI development and regulation.
• The proposal emphasizes the need for international cooperation to address challenges posed by rapidly advancing Artificial Intelligence, including bias, privacy concerns, and autonomous weapons.
• India highlighted its 'AI for All' approach, advocating for inclusive AI development that benefits humanity while mitigating risks.
• The summit would seek to build consensus on common principles, standards, and best practices for responsible AI innovation and deployment.
• This initiative positions India as a key player in shaping the global discourse on AI governance and ensuring a human-centric approach to technology.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Cyber security
prelimsPoints: India proposed hosting a Global AI Governance Summit.
Aims for unified framework for ethical AI development/regulation.
Emphasizes 'AI for All' approach.
Focus on bias, privacy, autonomous weapons in AI.
Positions India as a key player in AI governance.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing need for a global framework for AI governance, considering the rapid advancements and ethical dilemmas posed by Artificial Intelligence.
Analyze India's 'AI for All' approach and its potential contribution to shaping an inclusive and human-centric global AI policy.
Examine the key challenges that a Global AI Governance Summit would need to address, such as algorithmic bias, data privacy, and the regulation of autonomous weapons.
Evaluate India's strategic interest and potential leadership role in multilateral discussions on AI ethics and regulation.
Suggest how international cooperation can balance innovation with the responsible and safe deployment of AI technologies.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized brain with interconnected nodes, symbolizing AI, surrounded by global network lines.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956, defining it as 'the science and engineering of making intelligent machines'.

highlights: Government launches National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS) to boost industrial competitiveness and future workforce.
Supreme Court upholds key amendments to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, balancing development with environmental protection.
India-France Strategic Dialogue concludes, emphasizing enhanced cooperation in Indo-Pacific security and emerging technologies.
MNRE releases new guidelines for production incentives under the National Green Hydrogen Mission, aiming to make India a global hub.
ISRO gears up for its first Venus Orbiter Mission, 'Shukrayaan-1', in mid-2025 to study Venus's atmosphere and surface.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS), consider the following statements:
1. The mission aims to integrate traditional craft techniques with modern manufacturing processes.
2. It will primarily focus on developing skills for the informal sector.
3. The mission operates on a Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect; NMAMS focuses on integrating cutting-edge technologies like AI, IoT, and robotics into manufacturing. Statement 2 is incorrect; while it aims for a future-ready workforce, its primary focus is on advanced manufacturing, which is more aligned with the formal industrial sector. Statement 3 is correct; the mission will operate on a public-private partnership (PPP) model.

prelimsMocks.question: The recent Supreme Court ruling upheld amendments to which of the following environmental acts?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Supreme Court today upheld the constitutional validity of several contentious amendments made to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding India's National Green Hydrogen Mission:
1. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is responsible for issuing guidelines for production incentives under this mission.
2. The mission primarily aims to promote the use of blue hydrogen in industrial applications.
3. India aims to become a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; MNRE issued detailed guidelines for production incentives. Statement 2 is incorrect; the mission explicitly focuses on 'green hydrogen', which is produced using renewable energy, not blue hydrogen (produced from natural gas with carbon capture). Statement 3 is correct; a key objective is to position India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.

prelimsMocks.question: 'Shukrayaan-1' is the name of India's ambitious mission to explore which celestial body?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced that preparations for its ambitious Venus Orbiter Mission, 'Shukrayaan-1', are in advanced stages for a mid-2025 launch.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for releasing the annual report on the implementation of the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999?
options: (b) Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today released its annual report detailing the implementation and enforcement of the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999.

mainsMocks.question: The National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS) aims to integrate cutting-edge technologies and foster a future-ready workforce. Discuss the significance of this mission for India's economic growth and global competitiveness, highlighting the challenges in its implementation. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NMAMS and its objectives. Discuss significance for economic growth (GDP contribution, innovation, FDI) and competitiveness. Explain how it addresses workforce needs (skilling, future jobs). Outline challenges like technology adoption costs, skill gap, infrastructure, and coordination across stakeholders. Conclude with its potential for 'Make in India'.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's recent decision on the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, highlights the persistent tension between environmental protection and developmental imperatives. Analyze the implications of this judgment for India's sustainable development goals, critically evaluating the balance struck by the court. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development; GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the SC judgment on FCA amendments. Explain the core tension between conservation and development. Analyze implications: positive for infrastructure/strategic projects, potential negative for forest cover/biodiversity. Discuss how amendments redefine 'forest' and exemptions. Critically evaluate the 'balance' – are safeguards sufficient? Refer to constitutional mandates (Article 48A, 51A(g)). Conclude on the need for robust EIA and compensatory afforestation to uphold sustainable development principles.

mainsMocks.question: India's push for reforms in the WTO's Dispute Settlement Mechanism (DSM) comes at a time when the global trading system faces significant challenges. Discuss the reasons for the DSM's paralysis and India's proposals for its revival. How does a functional DSM contribute to a stable and equitable multilateral trading order? (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate; Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly explain the WTO DSM and its current paralysis (Appellate Body vacancies, US blockage). Detail India's proposals (two-tier system, judicial independence, addressing overreach). Discuss how a functional DSM fosters stability (predictability, rules-based order, preventing unilateralism) and equity (protecting developing countries, fair resolution). Conclude on its importance for India's trade interests and global economic governance.

quickBytes.text: The National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS) targets increased manufacturing sector's GDP contribution.
The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, amendments redefine 'forest' and exempt certain land categories.
'Bharat Shakti' is a tri-services (Army, Navy, Air Force) exercise showcasing indigenous weapon systems.
FEMA (Foreign Exchange Management Act), 1999, regulates foreign exchange transactions in India.
India-France Strategic Dialogue focuses on Indo-Pacific security, defence, and emerging technologies.
National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to reduce production costs and make India a global hub for green hydrogen.
ISRO's 'Shukrayaan-1' is India's first mission to explore the planet Venus, slated for mid-2025.
Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) aims to create a seamless online platform for healthcare services.
SDG 4 focuses on ensuring inclusive and equitable quality education and promoting lifelong learning opportunities for all.
The WTO's Dispute Settlement Mechanism (DSM) has been largely non-functional since 2019 due to Appellate Body vacancies.

GS1.headline: Coastal Erosion Poses Growing Threat to Indian Coastlines: Ministry of Earth Sciences Report
GS1.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES) revealed an increasing threat of coastal erosion along significant stretches of India's coastline.
• The report, based on satellite imagery and ground surveys, identified West Bengal, Odisha, Kerala, and Gujarat as particularly vulnerable states.
• Factors contributing to erosion include rising sea levels, increased frequency and intensity of cyclones, anthropogenic activities like sand mining, and climate change.
• The MoES recommended integrated coastal zone management plans, nature-based solutions (e.g., mangrove plantation), and strict enforcement of environmental regulations.
• The findings highlight the urgent need for proactive measures to protect coastal communities, ecosystems, and infrastructure from the adverse impacts of erosion.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: MoES report highlights growing coastal erosion in India.
West Bengal, Odisha, Kerala, Gujarat are highly vulnerable states.
Causes: sea level rise, cyclones, sand mining, climate change.
Recommendations: ICZM plans, nature-based solutions (mangroves).
India has a coastline of over 7,500 km.
mainsPoints: Analyze the major causes and consequences of coastal erosion along the Indian coastline.
Discuss the socio-economic and ecological impacts of coastal erosion on vulnerable communities and ecosystems.
Evaluate the effectiveness of existing coastal management strategies and suggest integrated approaches for mitigation.
Examine the role of climate change and anthropogenic activities in exacerbating coastal erosion in India.
What measures can be taken to enhance the resilience of India's coastal regions against future climatic events?
GS1.imageDescription: A drone shot of an eroding coastline with waves crashing against the shore, showing visible land loss.
GS1.trivia: Mangrove forests are highly effective in preventing coastal erosion and acting as natural barriers against storm surges.

GS1.headline: NITI Aayog Discussion Paper Highlights Urban Planning Challenges in Indian Megacities
GS1.explainer:
• NITI Aayog released a discussion paper outlining the critical urban planning challenges faced by India's rapidly growing megacities, including issues of sustainable infrastructure and governance.
• The paper identified problems such as unplanned growth, inadequate public transport, housing shortages, waste management crises, and increasing air pollution.
• It stressed the urgent need for integrated urban planning, leveraging smart city technologies, and empowering local urban bodies with greater financial and administrative autonomy.
• Recommendations included promoting green urbanism, developing robust disaster resilience plans, and fostering citizen participation in urban governance.
• The discussion paper aims to solicit feedback from stakeholders to formulate a comprehensive national urban policy framework.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog paper on urban planning challenges in megacities.
Identifies unplanned growth, transport, housing, waste, pollution.
Recommends integrated urban planning, smart city tech, empowered local bodies.
Promotes green urbanism and disaster resilience.
Aims for a national urban policy framework.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted challenges of rapid urbanization and unplanned growth in Indian megacities.
Analyze the role of urban planning and governance in ensuring sustainable and livable urban environments.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government initiatives (e.g., Smart Cities Mission) in addressing urban infrastructure and service delivery gaps.
Examine the importance of citizen participation and local body empowerment for effective urban management.
Suggest innovative policy solutions and best practices from around the world to tackle India's urban planning issues.
GS1.imageDescription: A sprawling Indian megacity skyline with a mix of modern buildings and congested areas, symbolizing urban challenges.
GS1.trivia: By 2050, over half of India's population is projected to live in urban areas, making urban planning a critical policy area.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Amendments to Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India today upheld the constitutional validity of several contentious amendments made to the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, in 2023.
• The amendments primarily redefine 'forest' and exempt certain categories of land from the Act's purview, including forest land up to 0.10 hectares for public utility projects and land within 100 km of international borders for strategic projects.
• Environmental groups and petitioners had argued that these amendments diluted the protective scope of the original Act and could lead to large-scale deforestation.
• The court, however, emphasized the need for a balance between environmental protection and developmental imperatives, asserting that the new provisions include adequate safeguards.
• The ruling is expected to expedite infrastructure projects in critical border areas and facilitate certain developmental activities in non-notified forest lands.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court upheld amendments to Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
Amendments redefine 'forest' and exempt certain land categories.
Exemptions include small public utility projects and strategic border projects.
Ruling balances environmental protection with developmental needs.
Original Act passed in 1980.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's judgment on India's environmental conservation efforts and infrastructure development.
Discuss the ongoing debate between environmental protection and developmental needs in the context of forest laws.
Critically evaluate the 'balance' struck by the court and the adequacy of safeguards in the amended Act.
Examine the role of the judiciary in interpreting environmental legislation and its impact on public policy.
Suggest measures to ensure sustainable development while accelerating infrastructure projects, especially in sensitive ecological zones.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a legal book with a backdrop of a dense forest, symbolizing the judiciary's role in environmental law.
GS2.trivia: The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, was enacted to check deforestation and ensure that forest land is not diverted for non-forest purposes without prior approval from the Central Government.

GS2.headline: India-France Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Indo-Pacific Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-France Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming the strong bilateral partnership and focusing on enhanced cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.
• The delegations, led by National Security Advisors from both countries, discussed a wide range of issues including defence collaboration, counter-terrorism, space technology, and civil nuclear cooperation.
• A joint statement highlighted the commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, with specific emphasis on maritime security and joint exercises.
• Both nations reiterated their support for multilateralism and reforms in global institutions, including the UN Security Council.
• The dialogue also explored new avenues for collaboration in emerging technologies and climate change initiatives, solidifying their strategic autonomy partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-France Strategic Dialogue held in New Delhi.
Focus on Indo-Pacific cooperation and maritime security.
Discussed defence, counter-terrorism, space, nuclear energy.
Both nations support UN Security Council reforms.
Led by National Security Advisors.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving strategic partnership between India and France, particularly in the context of Indo-Pacific geopolitics.
Discuss the significance of bilateral cooperation in defence and space technology for India's strategic autonomy.
Examine how India and France are contributing to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
Evaluate the potential for further collaboration in emerging technologies and climate change initiatives.
What are the challenges and opportunities for India-France relations amidst global power shifts?
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and France displayed together, symbolizing bilateral cooperation.
GS2.trivia: France was one of the first countries to establish a strategic partnership with India in 1998, following India's nuclear tests.

GS2.headline: Government to Revamp Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) for Wider Adoption
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced a comprehensive revamp of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to address implementation challenges and accelerate its adoption across the country.
• The revamp focuses on simplifying the user interface, enhancing interoperability between digital health platforms, and strengthening data privacy and security protocols.
• Key initiatives include increased outreach campaigns, training for healthcare providers, and incentives for states to integrate their health systems with ABDM.
• The mission aims to create a seamless online platform for healthcare services, linking patient records, doctors, and healthcare facilities through unique Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA).
• The move is expected to improve accessibility, efficiency, and transparency in India's healthcare delivery system.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: ABDM revamp announced by Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
Aims to simplify UI, enhance interoperability, strengthen data privacy.
Creates Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA).
Goal: seamless online healthcare platform.
Expected to improve healthcare accessibility and efficiency.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of digital health missions like ABDM in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Analyze the challenges faced in the implementation of ABDM and how the proposed revamp aims to address them.
Examine the importance of data privacy and security in a comprehensive digital health ecosystem.
Evaluate the role of technology in improving healthcare access and reducing disparities in India.
Suggest further measures to ensure equitable adoption and effective utilization of digital health services across rural and urban areas.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission logo with interconnected digital health icons.
GS2.trivia: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was renamed as Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in September 2021.

GS2.headline: Report Highlights Persistent Challenges in Achieving SDG 4 (Quality Education) in India
GS2.explainer:
• A recent report by a leading think tank outlined persistent challenges India faces in achieving Sustainable Development Goal 4 (SDG 4) on quality education by 2030.
• The report pointed to issues such as unequal access to quality education, particularly in rural and marginalized communities, inadequate infrastructure, and teacher shortages.
• It emphasized the learning crisis, with many students not achieving foundational literacy and numeracy skills, despite improvements in enrolment rates.
• Recommendations included increased public spending on education, focus on early childhood education, teacher training reforms, and leveraging technology for equitable learning outcomes.
• The findings underscore the need for targeted interventions and robust policy implementation to ensure inclusive and equitable quality education for all.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: Report on SDG 4 (Quality Education) challenges in India.
Identifies unequal access, infrastructure gaps, teacher shortages.
Highlights the learning crisis in foundational skills.
Recommends increased public spending and teacher training.
SDG 4 aims for inclusive and equitable quality education by 2030.
mainsPoints: Discuss the progress and persistent challenges India faces in achieving SDG 4 on quality education.
Analyze the socio-economic factors contributing to unequal access and poor learning outcomes in the Indian education system.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government policies and initiatives (e.g., NEP 2020) in addressing these challenges.
Suggest innovative strategies and policy reforms to improve foundational literacy, numeracy, and overall quality of education.
Examine the role of technology and public-private partnerships in bridging the educational divide in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Children studying in a classroom, with a graphic overlay of SDG 4 icon.
GS2.trivia: SDG 4 has ten targets, including universal primary and secondary education, early childhood development, technical and vocational skills, and increasing qualified teachers.

GS2.headline: India Pushes for Reforms in WTO's Dispute Settlement Mechanism at Geneva Meeting
GS2.explainer:
• India reiterated its strong demand for comprehensive reforms of the World Trade Organization's (WTO) Dispute Settlement Mechanism (DSM) at a ministerial meeting in Geneva.
• India emphasized the need for a fully functioning, two-tier dispute settlement system that is accessible, fair, and equitable for all members, especially developing countries.
• The DSM has been largely non-functional since 2019 due to the inability to appoint new members to its Appellate Body, mainly blocked by the United States.
• India's proposal includes restoring the Appellate Body, ensuring judicial independence, and addressing issues of overreach and delays in dispute resolution.
• A reformed DSM is crucial for maintaining a rules-based multilateral trading system and protecting the interests of developing nations.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India advocates for reforms in WTO's Dispute Settlement Mechanism (DSM).
DSM has been non-functional since 2019 due to Appellate Body vacancies.
India demands a two-tier, accessible, fair, and equitable system.
Proposals include restoring Appellate Body and ensuring judicial independence.
WTO is based in Geneva, Switzerland.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a robust Dispute Settlement Mechanism for the effective functioning of the WTO and the global trading system.
Analyze the reasons behind the paralysis of the WTO's Appellate Body and its implications for international trade.
Examine India's stance and proposals for reforming the DSM, particularly from the perspective of developing countries.
Evaluate the challenges and prospects for achieving consensus on WTO reforms amidst differing interests of member states.
How does a weakened WTO impact India's trade interests and its pursuit of a rules-based international order?
GS2.imageDescription: The World Trade Organization (WTO) logo with flags of member countries in the background.
GS2.trivia: The WTO was established in 1995, building on the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) which had been in existence since 1948.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Strengthening India's Data Protection Framework
GS2.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Information Technology submitted its report, recommending further strengthening of India's data protection framework, particularly concerning cross-border data flows and data localization.
• The committee emphasized the need for robust mechanisms to protect citizens' personal data, especially in the wake of increasing digitalization and cyber threats.
• Recommendations included a clearer definition of 'critical personal data', stricter penalties for data breaches, and establishing an independent Data Protection Authority with adequate powers.
• The report also suggested a review of the existing Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, to align it with evolving technological advancements and international best practices.
• This move is crucial for building trust in the digital economy and safeguarding individual privacy rights.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Cyber security
Role of non-state actors in security challenges
prelimsPoints: Parliamentary panel recommends strengthening data protection.
Focus on cross-border data flows and data localization.
Suggests clear definition of 'critical personal data'.
Recommends independent Data Protection Authority.
Review of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a robust data protection framework for India's digital economy and national security.
Analyze the key recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee regarding data protection, including cross-border data flows and data localization.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive data protection law in a diverse and rapidly digitalizing country like India.
Examine the balance between individual privacy rights, state surveillance, and business interests in the context of data governance.
Suggest how India's data protection law can align with global standards while addressing unique domestic concerns.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized image of data flowing through digital networks, with a shield icon representing data protection.
GS2.trivia: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, was enacted after years of debate and multiple draft versions, following the Supreme Court's 'Right to Privacy' judgment in 2017.

GS2.headline: India to Host International Conference on Traditional Medicine Systems
GS2.explainer:
• India announced its plan to host a major International Conference on Traditional Medicine Systems later this year, inviting experts and practitioners from around the globe.
• The conference aims to foster global collaboration in research, standardization, and integration of traditional medicine (like Ayurveda, Yoga, Unani, Siddha, Homoeopathy - AYUSH) into modern healthcare.
• Discussions will focus on evidence-based practices, regulatory frameworks, quality control, and intellectual property rights related to traditional knowledge.
• This initiative underscores India's leadership in promoting holistic health approaches and sharing its rich heritage of traditional medicine with the world.
• The event is expected to facilitate knowledge exchange and explore pathways for the global acceptance and utilization of traditional healing practices.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: India to host International Conference on Traditional Medicine Systems.
Aims for global collaboration in AYUSH research and integration.
Focus on evidence-based practices, quality control, IPR.
Underscores India's leadership in traditional medicine.
AYUSH stands for Ayurveda, Yoga, Unani, Siddha, Homoeopathy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of India's traditional medicine systems (AYUSH) in contributing to global health and well-being.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in integrating traditional medicine into conventional healthcare systems.
Examine the importance of standardization, research, and evidence-based validation for the global acceptance of AYUSH practices.
Evaluate India's role in promoting traditional medicine on international platforms and its implications for cultural diplomacy.
Suggest policy measures to protect traditional knowledge and ensure ethical practices in the commercialization of traditional medicine.
GS2.imageDescription: A collage of symbols representing various traditional medicine systems like Ayurveda, Yoga, and Unani.
GS2.trivia: The World Health Organization (WHO) established its Global Centre for Traditional Medicine in Jamnagar, Gujarat, India, in 2022.

GS2.headline: India-Sri Lanka Fisheries Talks Remain Inconclusive on Persistent Issue
GS2.explainer:
• The latest round of bilateral talks between India and Sri Lanka on the long-standing fisheries dispute in the Palk Strait concluded without a definitive breakthrough.
• The talks focused on issues of illegal fishing by Indian trawlers in Sri Lankan waters, damage to marine ecosystems, and the livelihood concerns of Sri Lankan fishermen.
• India reiterated its commitment to finding a humane and practical solution, proposing joint patrolling and alternative livelihood schemes for Indian fishermen.
• Sri Lanka maintained its demand for an end to bottom trawling and emphasized the need to protect its marine resources and the livelihoods of its northern fishermen.
• Despite ongoing diplomatic efforts, the issue remains a significant irritant in bilateral relations, impacting the lives of thousands of fishing communities.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-Sri Lanka fisheries talks remain inconclusive.
Dispute in Palk Strait over Indian trawlers in Sri Lankan waters.
India proposed joint patrolling, alternative livelihood schemes.
Sri Lanka demands end to bottom trawling.
Issue affects bilateral relations and fishermen livelihoods.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and complexities of the India-Sri Lanka fisheries dispute in the Palk Strait.
Discuss the socio-economic and environmental implications of bottom trawling and illegal fishing in shared waters.
Evaluate the various diplomatic and economic measures taken by both countries to resolve the dispute.
Examine the humanitarian aspect of the issue, focusing on the livelihoods of fishing communities on both sides.
Suggest sustainable and equitable solutions to address the fisheries conflict, considering marine conservation and regional stability.
GS2.imageDescription: Fishing boats in the Palk Strait, with maps showing the disputed maritime boundary between India and Sri Lanka.
GS2.trivia: The Palk Strait is a strait between the Tamil Nadu state of India and the Jaffna District of the Northern Province of the island nation of Sri Lanka.

GS2.headline: NITI Aayog to Develop National Gender Equality Index for Policy Tracking
GS2.explainer:
• NITI Aayog announced its plan to develop a comprehensive National Gender Equality Index to systematically track India's progress on various parameters of gender equality.
• The index will measure performance across key domains such as health, education, economic participation, political representation, and protection against violence.
• It aims to provide a data-driven framework for states and union territories to identify gaps, formulate targeted policies, and monitor the impact of gender-specific interventions.
• The initiative aligns with India's commitment to Sustainable Development Goal 5 (Gender Equality) and will serve as a crucial tool for evidence-based policymaking.
• This move is expected to foster greater accountability and accelerate efforts towards achieving gender parity across all sectors.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog developing National Gender Equality Index.
Measures progress on health, education, economy, politics, safety.
Provides data-driven framework for states/UTs.
Aligns with SDG 5 (Gender Equality).
Aims for greater accountability and gender parity.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a National Gender Equality Index in measuring and accelerating India's progress towards gender parity.
Analyze the key domains that such an index should cover and the indicators necessary for a comprehensive assessment of gender equality.
Evaluate the challenges in collecting reliable gender-disaggregated data and ensuring its effective utilization for policymaking.
Examine how this index can contribute to evidence-based policymaking and foster greater accountability among states and union territories.
Suggest complementary policy interventions and societal changes required to achieve true gender equality in India beyond mere statistical improvements.
GS2.imageDescription: A silhouette of a diverse group of people, symbolizing gender equality and inclusivity, with a bar graph showing progress.
GS2.trivia: The Gender Inequality Index (GII) is an index for measurement of gender disparity that was introduced in 2010 by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).

GS3.headline: Government Unveils National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS)
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the launch of the National Mission on Advanced Manufacturing and Skilling (NMAMS) aimed at boosting industrial competitiveness and creating a future-ready workforce.
• NMAMS will focus on integrating cutting-edge technologies like AI, IoT, and robotics into manufacturing processes, coupled with skill development programs for youth.
• Key objectives include enhancing productivity, promoting innovation, attracting foreign investment, and establishing Centers of Excellence across various industrial corridors.
• The mission targets a significant increase in the manufacturing sector's contribution to GDP and aims to create millions of skilled jobs over the next five years.
• It will operate on a public-private partnership (PPP) model, encouraging industry collaboration with academic institutions and government bodies.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Industrial policy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Skill Development
prelimsPoints: NMAMS aims to integrate AI, IoT, robotics in manufacturing.
Focus on skill development for future industries.
Operates on a Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model.
Key objective: increase manufacturing sector's GDP contribution.
Establishment of Centers of Excellence.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of advanced manufacturing for India's economic growth and global competitiveness.
Analyze the role of skill development in leveraging new technologies for industrial transformation.
Evaluate the potential impact of NMAMS on employment generation and youth empowerment.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in implementing a mission-mode approach for industrial modernization.
Critically assess the PPP model's effectiveness in driving technological innovation and skill development.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration depicting advanced manufacturing robots and skilled workers in a modern factory setting.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Industry 4.0' was first coined in Germany in 2011 to describe the fourth industrial revolution.

GS3.headline: India Successfully Concludes 'Bharat Shakti' Tri-Services Exercise in Rajasthan
GS3.explainer:
• The fourth edition of 'Bharat Shakti', a massive tri-services live fire and maneuver exercise, concluded successfully at the Pokhran Field Firing Ranges in Rajasthan.
• The exercise showcased the integrated combat capabilities of the Indian Army, Navy, and Air Force, demonstrating synergy in multi-domain operations.
• Key highlights included live demonstrations of indigenous weapon systems, advanced surveillance technologies, and joint logistics operations.
• The event was witnessed by senior defence officials and international observers, underscoring India's growing defence manufacturing prowess and strategic readiness.
• 'Bharat Shakti' aims to validate operational concepts, enhance interoperability, and provide a platform for showcasing 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiatives in defence.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
